meta.description: Curated reference data for the Sustainable Farming Assistant. Each entry carries a real source link to an agricultural-extension, university, or RHS publication, verified June 2026.
meta.disclaimer: Growing advice is regional. Which pests are present, when crops are sown, and which treatments are legal all vary by country and climate. The soil categories mix USDA texture classes with UK garden descriptors (peat, chalk). Treat this as a verified starting point and confirm specifics with your local agricultural extension service.

sources.name: Colorado State University Extension — Estimating Soil Texture

Sandy.characteristics: Light and gritty, drains quickly, warms early in spring, but holds little water or nutrient.
Sandy.drainage: Fast — can dry out and leach nutrients.
amendments: Dig in plenty of compost or well-rotted manure to build water and nutrient retention
Mulch the surface to slow evaporation
Feed little and often, since nutrients wash through quickly
tips: Great for early crops because it warms fast
Water more frequently but in smaller amounts

Clay.characteristics: Heavy and sticky when wet, hard when dry. Nutrient-rich but slow to warm and prone to compaction.
Clay.drainage: Slow — can waterlog in winter and crack in summer.
amendments: Work in coarse organic matter and horticultural grit to open up structure
Add gypsum to help break up heavy or sodic clay
Build raised beds to improve drainage
tips: Never dig or walk on it when wet — that compacts it badly
Holds nutrients well once its structure is improved

Loamy.characteristics: A balanced mix of sand, silt, and clay. The ideal soil: fertile, moisture-retentive, and well-draining.
Loamy.drainage: Excellent balance of retention and drainage.
amendments: Top up annually with compost to maintain fertility
Mulch to protect structure and feed soil life
tips: The benchmark every other soil is improved toward
Rotate crops to keep it balanced and pest-free

Silt.characteristics: Smooth and fertile, retains moisture well, but particles are fine so it can compact and crust over.
Silt.drainage: Moderate — better than clay, can cap on the surface.
amendments: Add coarse organic matter to bind particles and improve structure
Avoid compacting the surface; use beds and paths to limit treading
tips: A light mulch stops the surface from crusting after rain

Peaty.characteristics: Dark, spongy, and rich in organic matter. Holds moisture well and warms quickly, but tends to be acidic and short on some nutrients.
Peaty.drainage: Moisture-retentive; low-lying peat can become waterlogged.
amendments: Add lime to raise pH for crops that dislike acidity
Supplement with a balanced organic feed
tips: Ideal for acid-loving crops like blueberries
Warms early, giving a head start in spring

Chalky.characteristics: Pale, stony, and free-draining, often shallow over rock. Alkaline, which can lock up iron and manganese and yellow some plants.
Chalky.drainage: Fast — dries out quickly in summer.
amendments: Dig in bulky organic matter to hold moisture and nutrients
Choose lime-tolerant plants rather than fighting the pH
Use chelated iron for plants showing yellowing (chlorosis)
tips: Won't suit acid-lovers like blueberries or rhododendrons
Mulch heavily to offset fast drainage

pests.name: Aphids
pests.signs: Clusters of tiny green, black, or grey insects on shoots and leaf undersides, sticky honeydew, curling leaves, and sometimes sooty mould.
organicTreatment: Dislodge them with a strong jet of water
Spray insecticidal soap or horticultural oil on undersides
Encourage lady beetles and lacewings, which prey on them
prevention: Avoid over-feeding nitrogen, which spurs soft, aphid-friendly growth
Plant flowers nearby to attract beneficial insects

pests.name: Cabbage caterpillars
pests.signs: Ragged holes in leaves, green caterpillars on undersides, and dark droppings.
organicTreatment: Pick caterpillars off by hand
Apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt), a targeted organic spray
Cover crops with fine insect netting
prevention: Net brassicas before the white butterflies start laying
Encourage Trichogramma parasitic wasps with flowering plants

pests.name: Slugs and snails
pests.signs: Irregular holes in leaves, silvery slime trails, and seedlings disappearing overnight.
organicTreatment: Hand-pick after dark or on damp evenings
Set beer traps with the rim flush to the soil, or use copper barriers
Scatter iron-phosphate pellets, which are safer for pets and wildlife
prevention: Clear hiding spots like debris and dense ground cover
Water in the morning so soil is drier by night

pests.name: Colorado potato beetle
pests.signs: Yellow-and-black striped beetles, plump orange larvae, and rapidly stripped foliage.
organicTreatment: Hand-pick adults, larvae, and orange egg clusters into soapy water
Apply Bt (tenebrionis strain) to young larvae
Use floating row covers early in the season
prevention: Rotate potatoes and their relatives to a new bed each year
Mulch deeply with straw to make it harder for beetles to reach plants

pests.name: Tomato hornworm
pests.signs: Large green caterpillars with a tail-like horn, stripped stems, and dark droppings below.
organicTreatment: Hand-pick them (look for droppings to find them)
Apply Bt while caterpillars are small
Leave any covered in white cocoons — those are parasitic wasps doing the job for you
prevention: Cultivate lightly at season end to disrupt pupae in the soil
Encourage parasitic wasps with flowering herbs

pests.name: Spider mites
pests.signs: Fine webbing on leaves and a stippled, yellowing, dusty look — worst in hot, dry spells.
organicTreatment: Raise humidity and spray plants with water
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides, which kill the mites' predators and worsen outbreaks
Introduce predatory mites in greenhouses
prevention: Keep plants well watered and not heat-stressed
Remove dusty weeds and debris where mites build up

pests.name: Flea beetles
pests.signs: Many tiny shot-hole pits across leaves, and small beetles that jump when disturbed.
organicTreatment: Cover seedlings with row fabric from planting until flowering
Dust leaves with kaolin clay to deter feeding
prevention: Raise strong seedlings that can outgrow early damage
Keep beds weed-free to remove alternate hosts

pests.name: Carrot fly
pests.signs: Rusty-brown tunnels through the roots, and stunted, reddish foliage.
organicTreatment: Surround or cover the crop with fine insect mesh at least 60cm tall
Lift affected roots promptly to limit spread
prevention: Sow less-susceptible cultivars such as Flyaway or Resistafly
Avoid thinning on still evenings — the bruised smell attracts the fly
Rotate crops, since the fly overwinters in the soil

pests.name: Powdery mildew
pests.signs: A white, powdery coating on leaves and stems, usually starting on older growth.
organicTreatment: Remove the worst-affected leaves
Spray an OMRI-listed potassium bicarbonate product (e.g. Kaligreen, MilStop)
Improve airflow by spacing and thinning plants
prevention: Water at the base, not over the leaves
Choose resistant varieties and don't crowd plants

pests.name: Downy mildew
pests.signs: Yellow patches on upper leaf surfaces with greyish or purple fuzz underneath.
organicTreatment: Remove and destroy infected leaves (don't compost them)
Increase spacing and airflow
Grow resistant varieties as the main line of defense
prevention: Water early in the day at soil level
Rotate crops and clear debris

pests.name: Late blight
pests.signs: Dark, water-soaked blotches on leaves and stems, white mould underneath, and rapid collapse in damp weather.
organicTreatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately — never compost them
Harvest healthy produce early if blight is spreading
prevention: Grow blight-resistant varieties and use certified disease-free seed potatoes
Space for airflow and water the soil, not the foliage
Rotate away from tomatoes and potatoes for about three years

pests.name: Blossom end rot
pests.signs: A sunken, dark, leathery patch at the blossom (bottom) end of the fruit.
organicTreatment: This is a calcium-uptake disorder, not an infection — no spray cures it
Keep watering steady and even, and mulch to hold moisture
prevention: Water consistently rather than in feast-or-famine cycles
Maintain soil pH around 6.3 to 6.8 and avoid excess nitrogen

Legumes.role: Fix nitrogen from the air into the soil, leaving it richer for the next crop.
Legumes.feeder: Light feeder that improves the soil
Legumes.avoid: Following another legume in the same bed
Legumes.notes: Leave the roots in the ground after harvest so the captured nitrogen stays in the soil.

Brassicas.role: Hungry leafy crops that make the most of the nitrogen left by legumes.
Brassicas.avoid: Following another brassica, which lets clubroot build up in the soil
Brassicas.notes: Lime acidic soil before planting to discourage clubroot.

Roots.role: Reach into the soil for nutrients and help break up lower layers.
Roots.avoid: Freshly manured ground, which makes roots fork and split
Roots.notes: Follow brassicas, when the heavy feeding is done and the soil has settled.

Fruiting.role: Heavy feeders that benefit from rich, well-manured ground.
Fruiting.avoid: Following potatoes or tomatoes too closely, which spreads blight
Fruiting.notes: Manure this bed well before planting; the legumes that follow will restore nitrogen.
